seoTitle: Split a Transaction in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: How to divide a single bank transaction across multiple categories or accounts in QuickBooks Online using the built-in split tool.
intro: A single purchase — like a mixed office supply run — often belongs in more than one expense category. QuickBooks Online's split tool lets you break one bank line into several categorized rows without leaving the transaction screen, keeping your reports accurate down to the item level.

steps.heading: Open your bank transactions
steps.body: Go to Transactions, then Bank Transactions, and select the account holding the entry you want to split.

steps.heading: Open the transaction row
steps.body: Click the target row to expand the inline editing panel, switching from Match to Categorize if that's the default state shown.

steps.heading: Click Split
steps.body: Use the Split link next to the category selector to open the multi-row split view.

steps.heading: Assign categories and amounts
steps.body: Pick a category and amount for the first portion, then a second category for the remainder — QuickBooks will auto-calculate what's left.

steps.heading: Verify the balance and save
steps.body: Check that the Difference field reads zero, add a memo note for clarity, then click Split and Add to post the entry.

faq.question: How many categories can one transaction be split into?
faq.answer: You're not limited to two — you can keep adding rows as long as the amounts add up to the original transaction total.

faq.question: What happens if my split amounts don't add up?
faq.answer: QuickBooks flags this with a nonzero Difference value at the bottom of the split screen, so you can't save until the math balances.

faq.question: Can I split a transaction after it's already been categorized?
faq.answer: Yes, you can go back into a previously categorized entry and use the split option to break it apart further.

faq.question: Does splitting affect how the transaction appears on my bank statement?
faq.answer: No, your bank statement is unaffected — the split only changes how the single line is recorded inside your QuickBooks ledger.
